summary: A brief 2007 abstract by Stacey Gabriel outlining the opportunity to use population genetic tools and the reference human genome to explore the genetic basis of cancer. She advocates for combining large-scale studies of germline mutations with genomic technologies to identify somatic alterations.

principles.statement: Understanding common mutations in distinct cancers is critical for advancing precision medicine.
principles.rationale: It enables the identification of new drug targets, patient stratification for clinical trials, and the delineation of patient classes based on drug response.
principles.source_quote: An understanding of the common mutations that can cause distinct human cancers will be critical for identifying new targets for drug discovery, patient stratification for clinical trials, and analysis of drug response data to delineate classes of patients that respond to therapy.

principles.statement: A comprehensive understanding of the cancer genome requires examining both germline and somatic mutations.
principles.rationale: Combining large-scale case-control or family studies (for germline) with state-of-the-art genomic technologies (for somatic alterations) provides a complete picture of cancer's genomic structure.
principles.source_quote: The genome structure of cancer can be examined in several ways: (1) large-scale case-control or family studies can investigate germline mutations; and (2) state-of-the-art genomic technologies (eg, high-density oligonucleotide arrays and targeted re-sequencing), can identify somatic alterations. Combined, these approaches will lead to a better understanding of the cancer genome.

signature_quotes.text: The availability of a reference human genome sequence, an increasingly dense catalog, knowledge of common genetic variation, and new developments in technology present an unprecedented opportunity to systematically explore the genetic basis of complex human diseases such as cancer.
signature_quotes.context: On the opportunity presented by early genomic tools.

signature_quotes.text: An understanding of the common mutations that can cause distinct human cancers will be critical for identifying new targets for drug discovery, patient stratification for clinical trials, and analysis of drug response data to delineate classes of patients that respond to therapy.
signature_quotes.context: On the clinical applications of cancer genomics.
